items.why: Students use English -s plural. German has multiple plural patterns including UMLAUT + er for many masculine nouns: Mann to Männer, Buch to Bücher, Land to Länder. Plurals must often be memorised.

items.q: What is the German word for 'Christmas'?
items.why: Students struggle with Weihnachten spelling - the 'ei' and 'ch' and double 'a' confuse. Also it grammatically behaves as a plural noun (zu Weihnachten = at Christmas). Other festival terms: Ostern (Easter), Silvester (New Year's Eve).

A devastating faux ami. Bekommen looks like 'become' but means RECEIVE. 'Ich bekomme ein Geschenk' = I receive a present. To become = WERDEN. Confusion: 'I became a present' instead of 'I received a present'.

items.q: Which relative pronoun: 'die Frau, ___ ich liebe'?
items.why: It's die. “der” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
It's Goethe. “Schiller” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In what year was Faust I published?
items.why: Exact answer: 1808. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
